findings.description: Session tokens are generated with the non-cryptographic random module (Mersenne Twister), making tokens predictable and enabling session hijacking; use the secrets module instead.
touch() reads directly from the dict without purging, so it can renew (resurrect) an already-expired session that has not yet been purged, inconsistent with get().
user_sessions() does not purge expired entries, so it returns stale/expired sessions, inconsistent with get() and active_count() which purge first.
_purge_expired() does a full O(n) scan over all sessions on every get()/active_count() call, degrading lookup latency under high session counts.
The store mutates a plain dict with no locking, so concurrent access from multiple request threads can race (check-then-act, mutation during iteration) in a typical concurrent session-store usage.
create() does not check for an existing token, so a (astronomically rare) token collision would silently overwrite another user's session.
Token lookup via dict.get is not constant-time, allowing a theoretical timing side-channel on token validation.
Mixes legacy Optional[...] typing with modern builtin generics (dict[...]/list[...]) for inconsistent style.
